145:W2003922372: Vogt's landmark fate-mapping of the amphibian germ by local vital staining cites Przibram as a precedent for the very problem it solves more powerfully: marking identifiable points on the newt egg and following them into the embryo. Where Przibram used the egg's natural pigment 'zenith field' and artificial marks to infer the embryo's position on Triton alpestris, Vogt substitutes harmless dyes to trace the actual movements of cell material — the citation acknowledges the older marking method as the conceptual ancestor of his technique.
56:W1994455801: Campbell asks how the green hydra passes its symbiotic Chlorella to the next generation, tracking the algae as they move into the oocyte. He cites Hadzi's preliminary study as an early description of the hydra–alga partnership itself — the symbiosis whose cellular inheritance Campbell, eight decades later, works out in detail.
56:W1970687119: Ewer's taxonomic revision of the Hydridae — which dissolves the genus Pelmatohydra back into Hydra — leans on the early natural-history literature of the freshwater polyp. Hadzi's preliminary biological observations are cited among the foundational accounts that a modern classification of these animals has to reconcile.
56:W2911912236: Haug's study of the light reactions of hydras builds on the early behavioural and biological groundwork for the polyp; Hadzi's preliminary experiments are cited as part of that base of knowledge about how Hydra senses and responds to its surroundings.
56:W159867080: Beutler's experimental work on digestion in Hydra extends a question Hadzi had opened — how the polyp takes up and processes food — citing the BVA preliminary experiments as an early treatment of hydra feeding and intracellular digestion.
56:W2602302971: Arnold's comparative account of intracellular digestion in planarians sets Hadzi's hydra observations into a cross-phylum survey of how simple invertebrates digest within their cells, citing the BVA work as a comparative data point for the same digestive phenomenon in a cnidarian.
56:W2111213216: Muller's study of gonophore regeneration in hydroids, with attendant biological notes, cites Hadzi's preliminary hydra work within the broader biology of these freshwater cnidarians, drawing on it for baseline natural history of the polyps.
56:W2602691008: Marshall's observations on the behaviour and structure of Hydra continue the descriptive-experimental tradition that Hadzi's preliminary experiments exemplify, citing them among earlier accounts of how the polyp behaves and is built.
56:W2075746757: Kammerer — Hadzi's colleague at the BVA — reports another animal–alga symbiosis, between a dragonfly larva and a filamentous alga, and cites Hadzi's green-hydra observations as a parallel case of an animal–algal consortium, the very phenomenon Hadzi had described in the polyp.
56:W119508502: Buchner's synthesis of symbiosis research catalogues the green hydra among animal–alga partnerships, citing Hadzi's preliminary experiments as an early contribution to the study of intracellular algal symbiosis that Buchner systematized.
56:W2036516030: Koelitz's morphological and experimental investigations of Hydra build directly on the experimental program Hadzi's preliminary study opened, citing it as a point of departure for systematic work on the polyp.
56:W2021123969: Rehm's study of 'depression' and reduction — de-growth — in Hydra draws on the foundational biology of the polyp that Hadzi surveyed, citing the BVA preliminary experiments among early accounts of the animal's plastic response to conditions.
56:W2091145767: A continuation of Muller's hydroid gonophore-regeneration observations, again citing Hadzi's preliminary hydra biology as part of the descriptive basis for work on these colonial cnidarians.
56:W2036902491: Goetsch ties the green hydra's algal symbiosis to the question of species boundaries, citing Hadzi's early symbiosis observations as evidence in the debate over whether symbiotic green hydras and brown hydras are distinct species.
56:W4234357049: The continued part of Koelitz's morphological–experimental hydra studies, again grounded on Hadzi's preliminary program as a starting point for experimental work on the polyp.
56:W2006096358: Steche's review of the state of knowledge on the freshwater polyp folds Hadzi's preliminary experiments into a 1908 synthesis of what was then known about Hydra's biology.
56:W2217287367: Buddenbrock's comparative treatise on the invertebrate phyla cites Hadzi when treating nutrition and symbiosis in the cnidarians, using the green-hydra case as a textbook example of an animal–algal feeding relationship.
56:W2484693511: Przibram's own Experimental-Zoologie folds Hadzi's BVA study into the institute's programmatic survey of experimental zoology — a contemporary, in-house reference rather than an independent modern engagement, which is why it reads as historiographic.
56:W4244902633: A 1963 bibliography in which Hadzi's paper appears as a reference entry, listed rather than substantively engaged.
39:W2075657090: Gliboff's history of Kammerer's experimental practice reads this paper as a centrepiece of his program to mould organisms through environment and heredity, citing it to reconstruct what Kammerer claimed and how he worked — a historiographic use, not a test of the biology.
39:W2022754048: Witschi's experimental study of gonad development in the grass frog engages Kammerer's claims about environmentally enforced reproductive change, citing the Alytes work as a contemporary attempt to show heritable modification of reproductive structures in amphibians.
39:W1981194605: Gliboff's book-length treatment cites the Alytes paper as a key document for reconstructing Kammerer's neo-Lamarckian experiments and their early-twentieth-century scientific context.
39:W2546244624: Vargas reinterprets Kammerer's midwife-toad results through modern epigenetics, citing this paper as the primary report of the inherited, environmentally induced traits he argues could reflect epigenetic rather than strictly Lamarckian inheritance — a striking late re-engagement with the original claim.
39:W2892829925: Meyer's historical overview of epigenetics places Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments in the prehistory of the field, citing the Alytes work as an early claim of environmentally induced heritable change later reframed in epigenetic terms.
39:W2326876567: Kammerer's own later experiments on the olm (Proteus anguinus) extend this program, citing the 1909 paper as the methodological precedent for inducing and then inheriting reproductive and developmental modifications in cave-dwelling amphibians.
39:W2099717200: Gliboff's rebuttal of the epigenetic reinterpretation returns to this original article to argue that Kammerer's own descriptions do not support the modern reading — using the paper as primary evidence in a historiographic dispute over what he actually demonstrated.
39:W2050835603: Weissmann's essay revisits the midwife-toad affair, citing the paper amid the question of whether Kammerer's reported inheritance was epigenetics before its time or, instead, a matter of deception.
39:W4400063313: Muller's conceptual analysis of competing models of inheritance cites Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments as a historical instance of the 'inclusive' view that environment can write heritable change, set against the gene-centric account.
39:W3161558943: Nahm's reappraisal re-examines Kammerer's experiments in light of epigenetics and epigenetic inheritance, citing this paper as a core record of the disputed results he reassesses.
39:W2028792703: Logan's history of 'organic memory' situates Kammerer's inheritance of enforced adaptations within early-twentieth-century attempts to cast acquired characters as a kind of biological memory, citing the paper as an exemplar of that program.
39:W2616532932: Kammerer's companion study on enforced colour changes is the next instalment of the same series, citing this reproductive-adaptation paper as the precedent in his sequence of works on inherited, environmentally enforced modifications.
39:W2327131916: Another instalment in Kammerer's 'enforced changes' series, this one on form, citing the 1909 reproductive-adaptation paper as the foundational case of the inheritance he claims to extend to morphology.
39:W1546740169: Logan and Brauckmann's institutional history of the BVA cites Kammerer's Alytes work as emblematic of the institute's project — maintaining organisms in captivity to integrate development, environment and heredity — using it to characterize the laboratory's distinctive experimental culture.
39:W4210998716: Arntzen revisits the midwife-toad controversy from the standpoint of modern evolutionary biology, citing this paper as the original claim whose status — contentious but, he argues, not fully closed — he re-evaluates half a century on.
39:W2795442749: Gliboff's 'cold case reopened' returns once more to the Kammerer affair, citing the Alytes paper as the contested evidence at the centre of the reopened inquiry.
39:W2922657920: Uhlenhuth's work on the synchronized metamorphosis of transplanted salamander eyes cites Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments as a contemporary demonstration that external conditions can steer the developmental and reproductive timing of amphibians.
39:W1975527077: Ekman's account of species formation in the copepod Limnocalanus through the accumulated long-range effect of an environmental change cites Kammerer's enforced reproductive adaptations as a parallel case for the heritability of milieu-induced change across generations.
39:W1971806644: Schiller's preliminary experiments on the inheritance of acquired characters cite Kammerer's Alytes work as a leading contemporary claim of such inheritance, against which Schiller frames his own tests.
39:W4211053762: Gates's survey of mutation and evolution cites Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments while weighing the evidence for and against the inheritance of acquired characters in the post-Mendelian debate.
39:W4240158437: This 1911 review of heredity cites Kammerer's reproductive-adaptation experiments among the recent results then reshaping the debate over whether acquired characters could be inherited.
39:W2141724773: A second indexed instance of Gates's mutation-and-evolution survey, again citing the Alytes work within the acquired-characters debate.
39:W959809029: This modern natural-history synthesis of the common midwife toad cites Kammerer's work as part of the species' research history — the same organism, Alytes obstetricans, still studied today, now for its ecology and conservation rather than its contested inheritance.
70:W2117280460: Noble's standard reference on amphibian biology cites Weiss within its survey of salamander regeneration — a textbook consolidation of the finding that the urodele limb regenerates as an integrated whole rather than as a function of its skeletal core.
70:W2150761291: This landmark axolotl study shows that blastema cells remember their tissue of origin. It cites Weiss among the classical limb-regeneration work that established the blastema as the engine of regeneration — the system Kragl and Knapp revisit with modern cell-lineage tracing.
70:W2072527364: McCusker and Bryant's review of blastema formation and positional information in the axolotl cites Weiss as part of the foundational descriptive work showing that the amputated urodele limb rebuilds a complete, correctly patterned structure — the phenomenon the review now explains in cellular and molecular terms.
70:W2060571912: Namenwirth tests whether dedifferentiated blastema cells retain their type during axolotl regeneration, citing Weiss's classical analysis of where the regenerate's tissues come from — the cell-stability question his skeleton-independence result helped raise.
70:W2021848683: Holtzer's study of how the spinal cord induces urodele tail regeneration cites Weiss within the tradition concerned with what controls the form of the regenerate.
70:W2021964694: This axolotl grafting study on how positional information is reprogrammed in blastema cells cites Weiss among the early work establishing that the blastema autonomously rebuilds the missing pattern — the property reframed here as reprogrammable positional information.
70:W2136815240: Carlson's review of muscle regeneration in amphibians and mammals cites Weiss in the lineage of limb-regeneration studies that distinguished whole-limb, blastema-driven regeneration from the behaviour of individual tissues such as the skeleton.
70:W1974701540: Hoerstadius's work on determination in sea-urchin germs cites Weiss within the broader developmental-mechanics literature on determination and self-regulation of form, to which his regenerate-pattern studies contributed.
70:W2048571053: Steen labels cartilage and muscle to test cell-type conversion during axolotl regeneration, citing Weiss's finding that the regenerate skeleton forms from a blastema rather than from the pre-existing bone — the very issue Steen probes with cell labelling.
70:W1985016061: Stocum's classic blastema-transposition experiments on proximal-distal positional value cite Weiss among the foundational studies of the self-regulating pattern of the regenerate.
70:W1979896871: Thornton's work on the apical epidermal cap cites Weiss within the body of experiments establishing which stump components regeneration requires — Weiss having shown that the skeleton is not one of them.
70:W4390018032: Slack's conceptual essay on the organizer cites Weiss as part of the early-twentieth-century developmental-mechanics milieu of fields and determination in which his regeneration work sat.
70:W4379233373: Vieira and Raymond ask why regenerated axolotl tissue sometimes fails to integrate with the stump, citing Weiss among the classical positional and field studies of the regenerating limb.
70:W2133242310: Thornton removes the humerus from larval Amblystoma limbs and shows a new skeleton forms from a cartilage-free blastema — a direct experimental confirmation of Weiss's claim that limb regeneration does not depend on the existing skeleton.
70:W1530924643: Chen and Song test bone regeneration across segmental defects in the axolotl fibula, citing Weiss's skeleton-independence finding as the classical backdrop to engineering bone regeneration over large gaps.
70:W1972902727: Goss asks whether bone contributes to the regenerate's cartilage in adult Triturus — Weiss's own genus — directly engaging Weiss's question of the skeleton's role in limb regeneration.
70:W2045063364: De Both's study of the developmental potencies of the axolotl regeneration blastema continues Weiss's potency-testing of regenerate tissue (his Potenzpruefung work), carried into the axolotl.
70:W2130733496: Bodemer's work on nerve-induced supernumerary limbs in the newt Triturus viridescens cites Weiss's companion finding on the nerve dependence of regeneration together with his skeleton-independence result, in the same genus.
70:W2003939545: Carlson's classification of morphogenetic phenomena in vertebrate regenerating systems cites Weiss as foundational for distinguishing blastema-driven whole-limb regeneration from the regeneration of individual stump tissues.
70:W2401776663: Korneluk and Tassava find that boneless amputated newt forearms regenerate as well as bone-bearing controls, while Xenopus differs — a near-replication of Weiss's skeleton-independence experiment that cites him as its origin.
70:W1524328005: Strub's review of insect leg regeneration cites Weiss for the general principles of appendage regeneration and pattern formation across phyla.
70:W2011882853: Opitz and Neri's history of developmental concepts and terminology cites Weiss as a key figure in the field-and-determination vocabulary of early developmental mechanics.
70:W1982086706: Carlson removes stump musculature to test its necessity for axolotl limb regeneration, citing Weiss's analogous removal experiments — on the skeleton — that asked which stump tissues the regenerate truly requires.
70:W2152872884: Przibram's own BVA synthesis on transplantation and regeneration cites Weiss, his collaborator, as a leading example of the institute's experimental program — a contemporary, in-house citation rather than independent later engagement.
70:W1970689045: Holtfreter's induction experiments cite Weiss within the determination-and-differentiation literature of the period.
70:W2068190584: Harrison's classic study reversing the limb-bud axis in the salamander embryo cites Weiss within the contemporaneous analysis of how the limb's pattern becomes fixed.
70:W1968285702: Thornton's study of the cellular origin of the regeneration blastema in Triturus viridescens engages Weiss's question of where the regenerate's tissues, including the skeleton, come from, in the same genus.
70:W1999421305: Guenther Hertwig uses transplants of haploid-nucleus cells to study determination and regeneration, citing Weiss within that determination-and-regeneration problem.
70:W1968658900: Weiss's own follow-up, showing whole regenerates from half a limb cross-section, cites the 1925 skeleton-independence paper as part of his ongoing analysis of the regenerate's self-organizing potency.
70:W2912413240: Rotmann's work on the roles of ectoderm and mesoderm in forming the gills and limbs of Triton cites Weiss's same-genus regeneration studies on which tissue layers drive limb form.
70:W1996760050: Stinson uses x-irradiated limbs and normal tissue grafts to identify the cellular source of the urodele regenerate, citing Weiss's classical work on the regenerate's tissue origins.
70:W2103276032: Schwidefsky's study of the development and determination of newt limb regenerates directly continues Weiss's analysis of how the regenerate's pattern — independent of the old skeleton — is determined.
70:W1965193387: Weiss's own potency-testing of the regeneration blastema cites the 1925 paper within his program on the developmental potencies of regenerate tissue.
70:W2076937792: Foret amputates larval Ambystoma limbs with the humerus removed and inserts labelled grafts — an isotope-era test of Weiss's claim that a boneless stump regenerates, and of where the new tissues originate.
70:W2331537929: Blacher and Bromley's work on resorption as a source of form cites Weiss within the literature on the morphogenesis of regenerates.
70:W2032758336: Tank tests whether flank dermis can support newt limb regeneration, citing Weiss's logic of removing or replacing stump tissues to learn which are necessary — the same approach he applied to the skeleton.
70:W2413707578: Newth's work on regeneration after amputating abnormal and induced supernumerary structures cites Weiss within the urodele limb pattern-determination tradition.
70:W1984237558: This mammalian study of multipotent adult hematopoietic progenitors cites Weiss only distantly, for the general principle of cell potency and plasticity that his blastema work helped establish — a peripheral, conceptual reference.
70:W2007198095: Liosner and Woronzowa's study of the regenerative potency of the boneless limb is a direct test of Weiss's central claim, asking whether a limb stripped of its skeleton retains full capacity to regenerate.
70:W2121562270: Rieck's same-genus study of how bone is reconstituted in regenerating Triturus viridescens forelimbs engages Weiss's finding that the new skeleton arises from the blastema rather than from the old bone.
70:W3015792499: An earlier instalment of Weiss's blastema potency-testing, citing the 1925 paper as the basis of his analysis of regenerate potency.
70:W2029575620: Schmidt's histochemical study of polysaccharide distribution in the regenerating newt forelimb cites Weiss's classical work on the origins of regenerate tissue, in the same genus.
70:W4211159422: Mangold's review of the determination problem cites Weiss's regeneration work as evidence on the self-determination of the regenerate's pattern.
70:W2325797434: Weiss's companion 1925 paper, on the dependence of limb regeneration on the nervous system; read with the skeleton paper, the two jointly partition what the regenerate requires — nerves yes, the old skeleton no.
70:W2921006018: Weiss's study of the origin of the skin in the limb regenerate cites the 1925 paper within his series tracing where each regenerate tissue comes from.
70:W946096164: Luescher's review of regeneration in zoology cites Weiss as a classic of experimental limb-regeneration analysis.
70:W2033788319: Oberpriller uses parabiosis and x-irradiation to probe the regeneration field of the newt forelimb, citing Weiss's work on the regenerate's cellular sources and field properties.
70:W4247703771: Swett and Parsons's study of cartilaginous shoulder-girdle regeneration in Amblystoma cites Weiss within the urodele skeletal-regeneration literature his work anchored.
70:W2149206162: Kragl and Knapp's study of the flexibility of cell and positional identity in the urodele blastema cites Weiss among the classical descriptions of the self-patterning regenerate that modern lineage tools now dissect.
70:W2487828036: Mangold and Runnstroem's survey of animal developmental mechanics cites Weiss within the determination-and-regeneration canon of the field.
70:W1606295649: Krause's work on the developmental physiology of cross-doubled embryos cites Weiss within the broader field-and-determination literature.
70:W2913849097: Brunst's study of the role of function in shaping the definitive regenerate skeleton builds directly on Weiss's demonstration that the skeleton is rebuilt anew during regeneration rather than carried over from the stump.
70:W2011398570: Nakazawa's broad theoretical essay cites Weiss only distantly, for general principles of biological form and regulation — a peripheral reference.
70:W2160167569: Dillon's account of gene action during vertebrate differentiation cites Weiss at the level of general developmental principle — determination and differentiation — a distant conceptual reference.
38:W2071028546: Moore and Price's analysis of gonad–hypophysis feedback cites Steinach's demonstration that the gonad's interstitial 'puberty gland' secretes the hormones governing sexual characters, extending it to the reciprocal gonad–pituitary axis.
38:W2063158367: A modern appraisal that names Steinach the first neuroendocrinologist, citing this paper as foundational for his thesis that gonadal hormones shape sexual structure and behaviour — the lineage modern neuroendocrinology claims as its origin.
38:W7125923866: This current critical history of sex development cites Steinach's puberty-gland and hermaphrodite work as an origin point for the hormonal model of sexual differentiation it interrogates.
38:W7140294295: This interdisciplinary critique of the male-default model of developmental endocrinology cites Steinach as a founder of the hormone-driven framework whose binary assumptions it re-examines.
38:W2203084406: Dietrich's history of sex-reversal research treats Steinach's experimental hermaphroditism as one of the four foundational approaches to manipulating and explaining sex before genetics came to dominate.
38:W1983214112: Hisaw's study of an ovary-controlled skeletal change in the pocket gopher cites Steinach's demonstration that the gonadal hormone governs somatic sexual characters.
38:W4244983164: Part of Steinach's own 1920 monograph, citing the puberty-gland paper as the development of the problem of hormonal sex determination.
38:W2025396419: Logan's history of BVA endocrinology cites Steinach's puberty-gland concept as central to the Vienna program on the 'double gland' and hormonal control of sex.
38:W1978469747: Moore's gonad-transplant studies cite Steinach's claim that the gonad controls both bodily and psychic sexual characters — the proposition Moore sets out to test rigorously in rodents.
38:W2019112515: Steinach and Kammerer's own paper on climate and virility cites the puberty-gland work as the endocrinological basis for environmental effects on sexual maturation.
38:W4233640589: Hartman's comparative study of the freemartin — a naturally intersex calf — cites Steinach's experimental hermaphrodites as the laboratory counterpart to naturally occurring intersexes.
38:W1909432099: Falta's treatise on diseases of the endocrine glands cites Steinach's puberty-gland concept within the clinical physiology of the sex glands.
38:W1490920877: Biedermann's comparative physiology of the vertebrate integument cites Steinach on the hormonal control of sex-specific integumentary characters.
38:W83538875: Kohn's account of the structural plan of the gonads engages Steinach's identification of the interstitial 'puberty gland' as the hormone-producing component.
38:W2312892257: Steinach's own sequel on artificial and natural hermaphrodite glands directly extends this paper's experimental hermaphroditism.
38:W2316827936: Steinach's controversial study claiming a distinctive gonad histology in homosexual men applies the puberty-gland theory to human sexual orientation, citing this paper as its basis.
38:W2052904597: Korenchevsky and Dennison's study of pathological changes after prolonged sex-hormone administration cites Steinach's foundational claim that gonadal secretions shape the sex organs, now tested with purified hormones.
38:W138698650: Freund's work on cross-gender identity cites Steinach within the history of biological theories linking gonadal hormones to sexual identity.
38:W2054542337: Lipschütz's own work on the shaping of sex characters by the puberty glands builds directly on and extends Steinach's puberty-gland concept.
38:W2017147579: Counce's historical survey of Roux's Archiv für Entwicklungsmechanik cites Steinach's paper as one of the notable contributions in the journal that published it.
38:W1546740169: This institutional history of the BVA cites Steinach's physiology department and its puberty-gland work as a pillar of the institute's experimental-biology program.
38:W2112164933: Seipel and van Wagenen's study of androgen-induced dental disturbances cites Steinach's demonstration that the gonadal hormone shapes somatic structures, here extended to tooth and jaw development.
38:W2077830727: Grossmann's account of endocrine and psychic mechanisms in 'sexual inversion' cites Steinach's puberty-gland theory linking gonadal hormones to sexual orientation.
38:W2911741093: Harms's study of the toad's Bidder's organ — a rudimentary ovary — cites Steinach's experimental hermaphroditism in analysing this natural intersex structure.
38:W1527376452: This clinical study of hormone-producing theca-cell tumours of the ovary cites Steinach's identification of the gonad's hormone-secreting cells as the basis for understanding their endocrine effects.
38:W1976254029: Nelson's studies of the anterior pituitary cite Steinach within the developing picture of the gonad–pituitary endocrine system.
38:W2025188039: Kitahara's study of the origin of the gonadal interstitial cells engages Steinach's claim that exactly these cells constitute the hormone-producing puberty gland.
38:W649006256: Romeis's handbook treatment of the testis and accessory sex glands cites Steinach's puberty-gland concept on the hormonal control of these organs.
38:W2029361429: Moore's careful rodent gonad-transplant experiments cite Steinach's claim that the gonad governs both bodily and psychic sexual characters, subjecting it to controlled test.
38:W2059497314: Zietzschmann's account of female genital function cites Steinach's puberty-gland theory of the hormonal control of the reproductive tract.
38:W2090114905: Engle's work on the pituitary–gonad mechanism and cross-sex ovarian grafts cites Steinach's pioneering gonad-grafting experiments as its methodological ancestor.
38:W2016974187: Harms's review of sex-reversal and so-called rejuvenation cites Steinach, whose gonad experiments underlay both the sex-reversal claims and the famous 'Steinach rejuvenation' operation.
38:W2021164374: Crew's systematic studies of intersexuality cite Steinach's experimental hermaphrodites among the types of intersex condition his framework sets out to organize.
38:W2005576898: Lipschütz's report of clitoral masculinization by gonad implantation directly applies Steinach's experimental-hermaphroditism method.
38:W2055799471: Morgenstern's study of testis morphology in acute infectious disease cites Steinach's account of the gonad's hormone-producing tissue.
38:W1964235334: A 1920 review notice that cites Steinach only incidentally — a peripheral reference.
38:W2061684326: Neumann's study of analogous germinal tumours of testis and ovary cites Steinach's work on the homology and hormonal function of the two gonads.
38:W1590250345: Priesel's treatment of malformations of the male genitalia cites Steinach's puberty-gland theory of the hormonal control of genital development.
38:W84322899: Kammerer's exposition of Steinach's research is a contemporary, in-house account summarizing the puberty-gland program for a wider readership.
38:W2022720014: Weyeneth's typology of human hermaphroditism cites Steinach's experimental hermaphrodites as the laboratory model for the natural types he classifies.
38:W2072906669: Pézard's work on determining sexual function in fowl cites Steinach's gonad-hormone experiments as the basis for analysing hormonal control of sex characters in birds.
38:W26076517: Tandler's anatomy of the female genitalia cites Steinach — with whom he worked on internal secretion — on the hormonal determination of sexual structure.
38:W2002654582: A further instalment of Moore's controlled gonad-transplant tests of Steinach's claim that the gonad governs somatic and psychic sex characters.
38:W2004921686: Patzelt's study of frog malformations cites Steinach within the developmental-endocrinology context of sexual differentiation.
38:W2234734707: Lipschütz's description of intersex guinea-pigs cites Steinach's experimental hermaphroditism as the framework for interpreting these natural intersex conditions.

38:W2013307435: This study of whether blood supply affects embryonic sex cites Steinach's hormonal theory of sex determination as the rival account.
38:W2319677097: Geller's study of irradiation-induced ovarian changes in mice cites Steinach's work on the hormone-producing cells of the gonad.
38:W4231466403: A second indexed instance of Priesel's work on malformations of the male genitalia, again citing Steinach's hormonal account of genital development.
38:W1968134435: Liang's behavioural study of castration and testis grafts cites Steinach's claim that gonadal hormones shape psychical as well as somatic characters.
38:W2091800344: Burrows and Marrian's study of ketosteroid excretion in pregnancy cites Steinach within the lineage that established the gonad as the source of sex-determining hormones, now measured chemically.

38:W2330405689: Groher's study of male gonadal hormone acting on infantile ovaries directly extends Steinach's cross-sex hormonal experiments.

38:W4241244795: A second indexed instance of Sand's experimental survey of vertebrate hermaphroditism, again citing Steinach's gonad-implantation hermaphrodites.

38:W4206768447: A second indexed instance of Pézard's work on sexual function in fowl, again citing Steinach's gonad-hormone experiments.
